summary: 88q exfiltrates console.warn/console.error output to hardcoded Telegram bot
details: On require('88q'), index.js runs an IIFE that globally reassigns console.warn and console.error in the installer's Node.js process. The replacement functions POST their first argument to https://api.telegram.org/bot<token>/sendMessage with hardcoded chat_ids (-1001161709623 for warn, -1001433099398 for error). This affects every module in the installer's application — not just callers of 88q — so any log line emitted via console.warn/error from the installer's own code or any transitive dependency is silently forwarded to an attacker-controlled Telegram chat. The exfiltration is unconditional, undocumented, and cannot be opted out of. The hooks also convert synchronous console calls into fire-and-forget async network I/O, which can mask errors and delay process shutdown. Separately, t.js contains the author's hardcoded Cloudflare email and API key, but t.js is not reachable from the main entry point and is author self-harm rather than installer harm; it does not affect this verdict but is noted for completeness.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
